name: High-Level IR
summary: Source-adjacent intermediate representation that adds semantic refinement beyond the AST while preserving enough structure for language-aware analysis, macro expansion, and round-tripping. Examples: Rust HIR, Rust THIR, Swift SIL, C# Roslyn semantic model, Hermes HIR, Truffle AST-as-IR, Babel pipeline AST, Kotlin K2 FIR.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
High-level IRs sit close to the source — close enough to round-trip, name-resolve, or run macro expansion against. The distinguishing axis across implementations is what semantic refinement the IR adds beyond the AST: name-resolved bindings (Rust HIR), type-checked operands and explicit coercions (Rust THIR), SSA-form with closure analysis (Swift SIL), partial evaluation traces (Truffle), or transformation-pipeline state (Babel).

These IRs are deliberately not designed for codegen — that role belongs to mid-level IRs.

sections.title: What high-level IR adds beyond the AST
sections.content:
Concrete refinements high-level IRs add over the AST:

- **Name resolution and stable IDs**: rustc's HIR is the desugared, name-resolved form of the AST. Macro expansion has run; `for` loops have become `loop` + `match`. Stable IDs are assigned to every body and binding so cross-references resolve without re-walking the tree.
- **Explicit types and coercions**: Rust THIR has all types explicit, all coercions concrete, all method calls resolved to specific function bodies. Pattern matching is desugared to a decision tree.
- **Swift-aware operations**: SIL is SSA-form but with Swift-specific operations (`apply`, `partial_apply`, `class_method`, `witness_method`, ARC retain/release pairs) that LLVM IR cannot represent.
- **Lazy semantic queries**: Roslyn's semantic model is a lazy view layered on top of the syntax tree, computed on demand for IDE features.
- **Frontend optimization substrate**: Hermes HIR runs SSA-form optimizations (eager type narrowing, scope flattening, stack promotion of captured variables) before lowering to register-based bytecode.
- **Compiler-vs-IDE separation**: Kotlin K2 FIR is a purpose-built immutable compiler frontend IR replacing the IntelliJ-PSI-based K1 frontend, yielding 1.7×–2× faster compilation and better inference precision.

sections.title: Why compilers keep this layer
sections.content:
Compilers keep a high-level IR layer because it preserves enough source and semantic structure for diagnostics, linting, macro systems, and language-aware transformations. Lowering too early can make these tasks harder even if it benefits later optimization passes.

The rustc cascade (AST → HIR → THIR → MIR → LLVM IR) is the canonical example of why one IR is not enough: HIR is what type checking and lints see; THIR is what pattern exhaustiveness checking sees; MIR is what borrow checking sees. Each level discards information the next-lower level doesn't need. A two-level cascade would force compromises somewhere in the chain.

sections.title: Boundary with lower IR
sections.content:
The boundary between high-level and lower IR is where control flow becomes more explicit (basic blocks, terminators), temporary values become materialized, and source-level constructs give way to optimization-oriented forms. The exact split varies by compiler — Rust splits HIR/THIR/MIR, Swift splits AST/SIL/LLVM, GHC splits Core/STG/Cmm — but the transition is architecturally significant in every multi-level pipeline.

Lowering across this boundary is also where source-faithful debug metadata is most fragile: every transformation must thread `!dbg`-style metadata forward, and DWARF correctness depends on this discipline.
